topic: An athlete doesn't make the team they expected to join
raw_output:
[story 1]

The acceptance letter felt heavy in Maya’s hands, the crisp paper a stark contrast to the churning in her stomach. She traced the embossed seal with a trembling finger, the room spinning slightly as she tried to focus on the congratulatory words. She had poured every ounce of her being into those grueling training sessions, sacrificing sleep and social life for this moment. Now, the silence of her small apartment amplified every uncertain thought. *This isn't it. It can't be.* She reread the rejection email for the third time, the familiar sting of disappointment tightening her chest until it felt hard to draw a full breath. When her coach called, his voice was measured, professional, but the casual way he spoke about the next roster made her skin prickle with dread. "We have other options, Maya. We need to be realistic." Maya just nodded, the movement stiff, the weight of unfulfilled expectation settling deep in her bones.

[story 2]

Liam kept catching himself staring at the empty locker room door. Every time he thought about the tryouts, a cold knot would tighten just beneath his ribs, making his palms slick. He replayed the final sprint in his mind—the perfect finish, the sheer effort—but the outcome remained stubbornly out of reach. He fiddled incessantly with the drawstring of his sweatpants, the rough fabric abrasive against his skin. "Maybe I just didn't hit the right timing," he muttered to himself, the words sounding hollow even to his own ears. The air in the hallway felt too thick, suffocating. He kept glancing at the clock, the seconds stretching into agonizing eternities. He needed to move, to do *something*, but his legs felt leaden, rooted to the spot by a pervasive sense of impending failure.

[story 3]

Sarah paced the length of her living room, her footsteps sharp and uneven on the hardwood floor. She kept stopping to press her fingertips against her temples, a futile attempt to soothe the rapid drumming beneath her skin. The notification flashed on her phone—a polite, impersonal message confirming she wouldn't be on the team. A wave of nausea washed over her, making her grip the edge of the coffee table until her knuckles were white. She wanted to scream, to lash out at the unfairness of it all, but only a dry, shaky exhale escaped her lips. She looked out the window at the busy street, feeling utterly disconnected, as if she were watching a performance she was no longer part of. The silence in the apartment was deafening, echoing the hollow space where excitement used to reside.
